content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Commissioners Lowenstein, Carpenter,
Brosnahan, Lapan and Quinn

Date : September 24, 1976

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION - Conflicts of Interest Division
Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief

Subject: Opinion Request of William Owen, City
Attorney of the City of Davis

The matters related to the advice given in the
subject request require careful Commission scrutiny. The
consensus among the staff follows the reasoning of the
following letter. However, the facts should be reviewed
carefully to satisfy the Commission that a councilman who
is a consultant of a firm whose work product is before the
city council for various decisions, may vote on such decisions.

DLS:jo

September 22, 1976


MR. William L. Owen
City Attorney
City of Davis
220 F Street
Davis, CA 95616

Dear Mr. Owen:

Since your meeting with Mr. Finney and Mr. Coshorn of
our staff, we have had occasion to review the information you sent
us on August 30, 1976 and to analyze further the potential finan-
cial effect arising out of the adoption of specific aspects of
the report entitled "Planning for Energy Conservation" by the City
Council of Davis. Having done so, we generally agree with your
analysis as espoused in your letter of July 19, 1976 to Mr. Coshorn
and join you in concluding that, based on the facts presented, it
is not "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a ma-
terial financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
"public generally," on living systems. Government Code Section
67103. Accordingly, Councilman Kopper is not prevented, by virtue
of Government Code Section 87103, from taking or participating in
the taking of decisions of the City Council to reconsider portions of the cited report. We offer no opinion with respect to
the legality under Government Code Section 1090 or any future
contracts between the City of Davis and Living Systems.

In closing may I say that the Commission and its staff
are appreciative of the time and effort expended by your office
concerning this matter. We realize that to a certain extent we
may have complicated the matter with our rather extensive requests
for analysis of several difficult factual questions. I trust,
however, that you can appreciate the apparent novelty of the instant
situation and, hence, the feeling that we should be on firm factual
ground in ultimately endorsing your conclusion that Councilman
Kopper may participate in decisions concerning the adoption of
various aspects of the resort "Planning for Energy Conservation."
It is refreshing to receive the type of cooperation and interest
manifested by your concern over this matter.

I trust this resolves your request of July 19, 1976.
Please call on us again if we can be of further assistance in this
or any other matter.

Sincerely,


Michael Bennett
Executive Director

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 837 · SACRAMENTO, 95814 · 11633 E STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance 445-7635     Economics 445-7635     Enforcement 445-7635

April 21, 1976

Mr. William L. Owen
City Attorney
226 F Street
Davis, CA. 95616

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-048

Dear Mr. Owen:

Thank you for your letter dated April 2, 1976, requesting
the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an opinion con-
cerning possible conflicts of interest. Since your letter
raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of
the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the
following informal comments will be helpful.

In response to your first question, the Political Reform
Act does not prevent the making of a contract.1/ Rather, the
question is the lawfulness of the participation by Councilman
Kopper in governmental decisions affecting Living Systems, an
employer of the Councilman.

Section 87103 defines "financial interest" to include:

... (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; ....

From the facts, you state it is apparent that Living
Systems is a "source of income" to Councilman Kopper. Further-
more, his immediate resignation from the employ of Living Systems
would not change this result since for the next twelve months
the company would continue to constitute a financial interest
under the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

We note that you have advised Mr. Kopper concerning the
provisions of Gov. Code §1090. Our response is specifically
limited to an analysis of the provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

William L. Owen
April 21, 1976
Page Two


     The Commission recently has adopted an emergency
regulation concerning "source of income" which states:

     Source of income, as used in Government Code Section
87103(c), shall not include a former employer. All
income from the employer was received by or accrued
to the public official prior to the time he or she
became a public official; the income was received in
the normal course of the previous employment, and
there was no expectation by the public official at the
time he or she assumed office of renewed employment
with the former employer.

                                    2 Cal. Adm. Code §18704

     It does not appear that this regulation is applicable to the
facts surrounding Councilman Kopper's employment. He did not
receive all income "prior to the time he became a public
official." Accordingly, Mr. Kopper should disqualify himself
from participating in decisions which "it is reasonably fore-
seeable . . . will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally," on Living
Systems.

     It is not entirely clear from your letter whether the
decisions you list in your second question will have a
"material financial effect" on Living Systems. I interpret
paragraph four on page three of your letter to imply that
Living Systems may reasonably foreseeably stand to profit from
these decisions. To the extent that the requirements of
"materiality" are present, Councilman Kopper should not participate
See generally, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702, "Material Financial
Effect," as it relates to a "source of income."

     I trust that the foregoing analysis will be of assistance
to you in analyzing the referenced matter. If you have any
further questions, please contact Kenneth Goshorn, an Attorney
in the Conflicts of Interest Division at (916) 322-6641.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman
DHL:pvp

Date Received 4-5-76
4-19-76

ARMY OF DAVIS

226 F Street Davis, California 95616
(916) 756-3740

April 2, 1976

REPORT MENTIONED IS
IN THE FILE.

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1153 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion Regarding
Conflict of Interest Provisions

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

I hereby request, on behalf of Davis City Councilman,
William D. Kopper, an official opinion concerning the following
questions relating to the Political Reform Act of 1974:

1. Is a contract for consulting services permissible
under Article 1 of Chapter 7 of the Political
Reform Act of 1974, where a member of the city
council permanently resigns his employment posi-
tion with the consulting firm prior to submission
of the consulting services proposal and where
such councilman abstains from all discussion and
action on such proposal?

2. May a councilman vote on revisions to zoning,
subdivision and community design regulations
based upon recommendations prepared by a consult-
ing firm which presently employs the councilman?

3. If the response to question No. 2 is negative,
would the response be different should the
councilman permanently resign his employment prior
to consideration of the proposed revisions?

FACTS (QUESTION NO. 1)

On March 2, 1976, William D. Kopper was elected to the Davis
City Council. Mr. Kopper is an environmental consultant.

()

Daniel H. Lowenstein
April 2, 1976
Page 2

In October, 1975, Mr. Kopper became a part-time employee of
Living Systems, an environmental consulting firm located in Yolo County
which specializes in energy conservation research and technology.
Mr. Kopper's particular duties with Living Systems have related to the
study of methods of economically retrofitting existing residences with
insulation and shading devices in order to save energy for heating and
cooling.

Mr. Kopper has earned approximately $2,250 from his employment
with Living Systems during the last twelve months.

On March 2, 1976, the voters of the City of Davis authorized
a general obligation bond issue to be used for park and recreation
purposes. One of the planned uses of the bond funds will be the design
and installation of solar energy heaters at two City owned and operated
public swimming pools. Approximately $60,000 has been earmarked for the
design and installation of the pool heaters.

Living Systems has expressed a desire to submit a proposal for
consulting services to the City in connection with the proposed pool
heater design and installation. Such proposal, if accepted, would
culminate in an agreement which would be submitted to the City Council
for approval.

I have advised Mr. Kopper that it is my opinion that Government
Code Section 1090 would prohibit a new consulting agreement 1/ with
Living Systems so long as Mr. Kopper is both an employee of Living
Systems and a member of the City Council.

Mr. Kopper has indicated that he may be willing to resign his
employment with Living Systems and abstain from voting or influencing
the vote upon such a consulting proposal if such actions would permit
Living Systems to submit a proposal for consulting services.

________________________

1/ Prior to Mr. Kopper's employment with Living Systems, the City of
Davis entered into three consulting contracts with Living Systems
for services in connection with (a) recommendations for local
energy conservation measures related to building and community design;
(b) study of energy conservation measures for a proposed civic
center; (c) a HUD funded contract to design two energy conserving
prototype residences and to monitor energy savings in houses built
in accordance with the City's own building design standards.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
June 2, 1976
Page 3

Of course, Mr. Kopper has and will continue to comply with
the reporting requirements of Chapter 7, including the listing of income
received from employment with Living Systems.

FACTS (QUESTIONS NO. 2 AND 3)

In 1974, Living Systems submitted a report to the City of Davis
entitled A Strategy for Energy Conservation - Proposed Energy Conservation
and Solar Utilization Ordinance for the City of Davis, California. The
"Strategy" was prepared pursuant to a consultant agreement between the
City and Living Systems, and jointly funded by the City and the University
of California. A copy of the "Strategy" is enclosed for your information.

Mr. Kopper assisted in the writing of Chapter I of "Strategy"
under an independent subcontract with the authors. Mr. Kopper received
approximately $700 for his contribution to the report from the funds
provided by the University of California.

A portion of the "Strategy" (Chapter IV) recommended adoption
of new building standards. Energy conservation standards, based in large
part upon the recommendations contained in Chapter IV, were adopted by
ordinance and resolution in 1975. A separate portion of the "Strategy"
(Chapter V) recommended certain changes in community design approaches
for energy conservation. The community design amendments to zoning,
subdivision and other ordinances have not yet been presented to the City
Council.

Due to the interest that other communities have expressed in
energy conservation, Living Systems has received inquiries from other
local agencies and has entered consulting contracts with at least two
agencies (County of Sacramento and City of Indio).

Councilman Kopper is interested and knowledgeable in energy
conservation methods and it is his desire, if legally permissible, to
vote upon the community design recommendations contained in Chapter V
of the "Strategy" as well as any modifications thereof recommended by
City staff and advisory commissions.

If you desire further information concerning the matters
mentioned herein, please contact me.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM L. OWEN
City Attorney

WLO:pb
enclosure
